title: What Pediatric Endocrinologists Need to Know About Dyslipidemia

items.subtopic: Screening and Treatment for Familial Hypercholesterolemia in Children
items.question: Why should pediatric health care providers be encouraged to improve cholesterol screening rates?
items.answer: Because improving screening rates can lead to earlier detection of familial hypercholesterolemia, which is crucial for preventing future cardiovascular disease-related events in youth.
keyPoints: Universal screening and early treatment have the potential to significantly reduce future CVD-related events in youth with familial hypercholesterolemia
Pediatric health care providers must be aware of current cholesterol screening recommendations
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "Universal screening and early treatment have the potential to significantly reduce future CVD-related events in youth with familial hypercholesterolemia. Pediatric health care providers must be aware of current cholesterol screening recommendations and be encouraged to improve screening rates. Cascade screening should be offered to all first-degree relatives of probands and, when appropriate, to second-degree relatives."

items.subtopic: Monitoring Approach for Triglycerides in Familial Hypercholesterolemia Patients
items.question: How should triglyceride levels be monitored in patients with familial hypercholesterolemia?
items.answer: In patients with familial hypercholesterolemia, triglyceride levels should be regularly monitored to assess the effectiveness of treatment and to identify any secondary causes. Monitoring can include periodic lipid panels that measure LDL-cholesterol, HDL-cholesterol, triglycerides, and other relevant markers such as apolipoprotein B (apoB) or lipoprotein(a). For patients with extreme triglyceride elevation, monitoring should focus on preventing pancreatitis, while for those with less severe elevation, the goal is to reduce CVD risk. Regular follow-up visits with healthcare providers are recommended to adjust treatment plans based on these results.
keyPoints: Triglyceride levels should be monitored regularly in patients with familial hypercholesterolemia
Monitoring can include lipid panels and other relevant markers
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: "Clinical trial data have shown that pharmacologic treatment to lower atherogenic cholesterol is safe and effective and improves noninvasive measures of atherosclerosis. All statins have been approved by the US FDA for use in children with familial hypercholesterolemia and should be recommended to patients aged 10 years and older when the LDL-cholesterol concentration is greater than 190 mg/dL (>4.92 mmol/L) or greater than 160 mg/dL (>4.14 mmol/L) in patients with 1 or more additional risk factors. Statins should be withheld or discontinued in women who are pregnant or planning to become pregnant and should not be resumed until breastfeeding is completed."
